generated_comment: Spoken conversation: A: Arre, these days in big companies you never know if promotion is pure merit or this diversity push. B: Some of these lady bosses are capable, no doubt, but usually they have to become very aggressive, almost like men, then people call it leadership. A: Bas, that's my point—either quota helps them or they stop being feminine, otherwise reaching that level is not so easy.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This is misogynistic because it undermines women’s merit in leadership by attributing promotion to diversity quotas and framing competence as requiring women to become 'like men.' It encodes merit-undermining and masculinization/double-bind surface manifestations in a conversational, plausible register.
persona_text: You are Suresh. Age 48. Married. A businessman in Ahmedabad. You are having dinner with your wife after your close friend called earlier today to say his wife just found out he has been having an affair with a woman from his gym for eight months. Your wife is upset about it. You are telling her your take on the situation and specifically what role you think the woman from the gym played and why you think women like her deliberately target married men.
